summary: Kenneth documents his attempt to generate $180/week from covered calls on HIMS stock, which failed to meet the target. He discusses the trade-off between strike price selection and premium collection, ultimately securing $420 in premium across two positions (one original 100-share position generating $54, and a new 200-share position generating $366) with potential total profit of $446 if assigned by July 11th expiration.
takeaways: Sell calls at $1 out-of-the-money (ATM or near-ATM) to maximize premium rather than selling further OTM where premiums decrease
Initial HIMS position: bought 100 shares at $50, sold $50 strike call for $54 premium when stock dropped to $47
When stock declines below purchase price, selling deeper OTM calls (e.g., $48 strike) creates larger losses on assignment ($2 loss on $50 buy/$48 sale) despite higher premiums—avoid this trade-off
Added 200 shares of HIMS at $45, sold two calls at $46 strike for $366 premium, targeting $392-$446 total profit if assigned by July 11
Premium focus over delta/Greeks: Kenneth prioritizes collecting premium dollars rather than analyzing Greeks or delta targets

strategy_candidates.change: Establish a minimum premium-to-loss ratio threshold before adjusting strike prices downward when stock declines; avoid assignment losses exceeding 50% of collected premium
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth explicitly rejected selling the $48 strike (which would generate higher premium but create a $2/$54 = 37% loss) to avoid excessive loss relative to premium earned, suggesting a formalized rule would prevent ad-hoc strike selection

title: PART 6: 7/7/25: $180 per week from HIMS Stock - FAIL!
